coredata.dc:title: Nature-derived carbon dots for ratiometric analysis: Bismuth-doped fluorescent probe for oyster shell determination in tablet dosage forms
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Hamed, Mahmoud
Ibrahim, Noha
Bedair, Alaa
Mansour, Fotouh R.
Emara, Samy
Zarad, Walaa
coredata.dc:description: Wheat bran (WB), a major byproduct of the milling industry, is the most prevalent among various cereal bran residues. The substantial accumulation of WB biomass is driving the research community to seek innovative and economically viable applications for this bioresource. In this context, the potential for utilizing WB byproducts as nature-derived carbon quantum dots (n-CQDs) precursor has been highlighted. This research introduces an innovative, environmentally friendly, and effective WB-based n-CQDs (WB-n-CQDs) synthesized by microwave-assisted method. The developed WB-n-CQDs-based fluorescence probe, in combination with silver nitrate, demonstrates high sensitivity and selectivity for oyster shell calcium (OSC) quantification. The incorporation of bismuth oxide and folic acid as dopants effectively enhances the intensity of two emission peaks at 354 nm and 445 nm, thereby improving fluorescence ratiometric capabilities for measuring OSC. A variety of experimental techniques has been employed to investigate the structural, morphological, and optical characteristics of WB-n-CQDs, including Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy, energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, X-ray diffraction, fluorescence spectroscopy, UV–Vis spectroscopy, and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy. The experimental results demonstrated remarkable physical and chemical characteristics, highlighting the potential of WB-n-CQDs for their applications in green analytical chemistry. The fluorescence intensity of Ag-WB-n-CQDs demonstrated an acceptable linear correlation with OSC concentration levels ranging from 0.1 to 1.0 μg/mL, along with high recovery percentages that lie between 98 % and 102 %. The successful application of Ag-WB-n-CQDs for measuring OSC in its dosage forms demonstrates the promising potential of biomass-derived n-CQDs in sustainable and advanced sensing technologies.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carvon dots
Ratiometric
Oyster shell
Dosage form
Green analytical chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Application of the n-CQDs method for dosage form analysis
Greenness and practicality assessment of the proposed method

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY license.

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Consecutive steps for the preparation of WB-n-CQDs using domestic microwave irradiation.

(A) Effect of different WB weights (g) on the emission and excitation of the WB-n-CQDs. (B) Effect of microwave irradiation time (sec) on the emission and Excitation of the WB-n-CQDs. (C, D) Effect of temperature time (min) on the emission at 354 nm after excitation at 275 (E, F) Effect of temperature time (min) on the emission spectrum at 445 nm after excitation at 338 nm of the WB-n-CQDs.
(I) Effect of doping on the emission of the WB-n-CQDs prepared from WBs after excitation 275 nm using folic acid alone and folic acid with bismuth oxide.
A) The HR-TEM image of the synthesized WB-n-CQDs at magnification power 10 nm and 5 nm, B) FTIR spectrum of the synthesized WB-n-CQDs, (C) XRD pattern of WB-n-CQDs.

Elemental mapping illustrates the distribution of (A) carbon, oxygen, sodium, phosphorus, sulfur, potassium, and bismuth in an overlay, followed by individual element maps: (B) carbon, (C) oxygen, (D) sodium, (E) phosphorus, (F) sulfur, (G) potassium (H) bismuth in WB-n-CQDs. (I) EDX elemental analysis of the fabricated WB-n-CQDs.
(A) XPS survey spectrum of WB-n-CQDs, high resolution scans of (B) C1s, and (C) O1s (D) N1s.
The bar chart shows the pH effect on the fluorescence emission at 354 and 445 nm after excitation at 275 nm.
The fluorescence emission spectra of WB-n-CQDs under varying concentrations of OSC (0.1–1.0 μg/mL).
Evaluation of the greenness and practicality of the ratiometric method using different matrices A) ComplexMoGAPI, B) AGREEprep, C) BAGI, and D) CACI.
The assessment of the accuracy and precision of WB-n-CQDs method.
Evaluation of the robustness of the method.

head.ce:title: Nature-derived carbon dots for ratiometric analysis: Bismuth-doped fluorescent probe for oyster shell determination in tablet dosage forms

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: the medical campus of Tanta University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Wheat bran (WB), a major byproduct of the milling industry, is the most prevalent among various cereal bran residues. The substantial accumulation of WB biomass is driving the research community to seek innovative and economically viable applications for this bioresource. In this context, the potential for utilizing WB byproducts as nature-derived carbon quantum dots (n-CQDs) precursor has been highlighted. This research introduces an innovative, environmentally friendly, and effective WB-based n-CQDs (WB-n-CQDs) synthesized by microwave-assisted method. The developed WB-n-CQDs-based fluorescence probe, in combination with silver nitrate, demonstrates high sensitivity and selectivity for oyster shell calcium (OSC) quantification. The incorporation of bismuth oxide and folic acid as dopants effectively enhances the intensity of two emission peaks at 354 nm and 445 nm, thereby improving fluorescence ratiometric capabilities for measuring OSC. A variety of experimental techniques has been employed to investigate the structural, morphological, and optical characteristics of WB-n-CQDs, including Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy, energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, X-ray diffraction, fluorescence spectroscopy, UV–Vis spectroscopy, and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy. The experimental results demonstrated remarkable physical and chemical characteristics, highlighting the potential of WB-n-CQDs for their applications in green analytical chemistry. The fluorescence intensity of Ag-WB-n-CQDs demonstrated an acceptable linear correlation with OSC concentration levels ranging from 0.1 to 1.0 μg/mL, along with high recovery percentages that lie between 98 % and 102 %. The successful application of Ag-WB-n-CQDs for measuring OSC in its dosage forms demonstrates the promising potential of biomass-derived n-CQDs in sustainable and advanced sensing technologies.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Oyster shell calcium (OSC), primarily composed of calcium carbonate, is a versatile material with wide-ranging applications in health, industry, and agriculture. In health, OSC serves as an effective calcium supplement when chelated with l-aspartic acid, forming l-aspartic acid chelated calcium from oyster shell (ACOS), which improves calcium absorption, enhances bone growth, and regulates bone metabolism by increasing osteoprotegerin and reducing receptor activator of nuclear factor kappa-B ligand levels [1]. This supplementation shows promise for treating osteoporosis, particularly in areas with high prevalence [2], and has demonstrated anticancer properties by reducing tumor formation and enhancing oral epithelial differentiation in mice, potentially preventing oral squamous cell carcinoma [3]. In industry and agriculture, calcium acetate (PhosLo gelcaps) derived from oyster shells is utilized as an eco-friendly deicing agent that avoids metal corrosion, unlike chloride-based alternatives [4]. It also contributes to sustainable agricultural practices as a soil amendment and calcium source in animal feed [5,6]. In the food industry, OSC enhances the textural properties of pork products as a replacement for synthetic phosphates [7]. Although generally safe, rare cases of mild adverse reactions, such as swelling below the ear, have been reported, which may necessitate discontinuation of use [8]. Additionally, oyster shell powder is used to produce natural calcium tablets for dietary supplementation. Despite extensive research on calcium detection, most studies have focused on using analytical techniques such as atomic absorption spectroscopy [9], ion-selective electrodes [10], and capillary electrophoresis [11]. However, these conventional methods often require complex instrumentation, labor-intensive sample preparation, and high operational costs, making them less practical for widespread use in environmental or industrial settings. While fluorescence-based assays offer advantages such as high sensitivity, rapid detection, and ease of use, their application in calcium analysis for oyster shells remains largely unexplored [12]. Therefore, there is a clear need to develop a simple, cost-effective, and reliable approach for calcium detection in oyster shells.

Nature-derived carbon quantum dots (n-CQDs), emerging as versatile carbon-based nanomaterials, exhibit remarkable properties such as high photostability, excellent catalytic activity, biocompatibility, and low toxicity, attributed to their zero-dimensional, point-like structure [13]. The synthesis and applications of CDs, particularly n-CQDs derived from natural resources, have attracted significant attention due to their environmental friendliness, abundance, and the inherent heteroatom content of their precursors. For targeted detection applications, it is essential to design n-CQDs with specific excitation and emission properties, improved fluorescence quantum yield (QY), and other tailored surface characteristics. To achieve this, various preparation strategies have been developed. Generally, n-CQDs are synthesized via two primary approaches: "top-down" methods, such as hydrothermal treatment, microwave-assisted synthesis, and pyrolysis, and "bottom-up" strategies [14]. These methods utilize natural precursors, including plant waste and food residues, to produce n-CQDs with tailored properties by adjusting synthesis parameters like temperature, time, and carbon sources [15]. The selection of synthesis methods and precursors enables the production of n-CQDs with diverse functionalities, enhancing their applications in fluorescence sensing, catalysis, biological imaging, and electrochemical sensors [16]. This growing field continues to face challenges in improving QY and scalability while unlocking new opportunities for n-CQDs-based technologies.

Ratiometric fluorescent sensors are utilized to improve selectivity and sensitivity, allowing for the determination of analyte concentrations through the measurement of emission ratios at two different emission wavelengths [17]. Unlike single-channel detection methods, ratiometric fluorescent sensors effectively circumvent numerous challenges, such as fluctuations in the optoelectronic system (including lamps and detectors), and variations in probe concentration, which can interfere with quantitative detection. Consequently, these sensors provide enhanced operational precision and accuracy. When developing a ratiometric fluorescence-based method, a primary focus is on fabricating a probe that possesses effective and suitable fluorescent properties. CQDs, which are a new category of fluorescent carbon nanomaterials that are increasingly utilized in analytical applications as prominent ratiometric fluorescent probes, offer numerous benefits, including controllable optical properties, water solubility, straightforward preparation, and easy surface functionalization [18]. Despite the growing interest in using natural products as a primary carbon source for the synthesis of n-CQDs, no n-CQDs-based fluorescent probes exhibiting dual emissions specifically tailored for the quantification of OSC have been reported to date. Wheat bran (WB) represents a major byproduct of the milling industry and is the most prevalent type of cereal bran residue. Nevertheless, research on the production of n-CQDs derived from WB is limited, particularly in relation to their biological properties and their potential role as a drug delivery system for amoxicillin [19,20]. Therefore, to effectively measure the concentration of OSC, it is essential to create a cost-effective, straightforward, stable, rapid, and reliable protocol using non-toxic fluorescent materials like n-CQDs. This study presents the synthesis of environmentally friendly and highly fluorescent n-CQDs through a simple microwave-assisted process using WB (WB-n-CQDs). The resulting WB-n-CQDs exhibit excellent fluorescence properties and are particularly suitable for measuring OSC. The incorporation of bismuth oxide and folic acid as dopants effectively enhances the intensity of the primary and secondary emission peaks for ratiometric measurement of OSC. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first method for the analysis of OSC. The method was based on the quantitative ratiometric fluorescence after the addition of AgNO3 and the analyte.

OSC (99.5 %) was sourced from Multicare Egypt Company (Cairo, Egypt). Sodium hydroxide and hydrochloric acid, nitric acid, silver nitrate were obtained from Piochem Company (6th of October City, Egypt). Quinine sulfate was procured from ISOCHEM Laboratory. Cal-Preg Multicare Egypt® tablets (batch number 240,719), manufactured by Multicare Egypt and labeled to contain 1600 mg of OSC per tablet, were purchased from the local market.

The fluorescence measurements were conducted using the RF-5301 spectrofluorometer (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan), which was connected to a PC and operated with fluorescence spectroscopy software version 2.04. The instrument settings included an excitation and emission bandwidth of 5 nm, a high scanning speed, and maximum sensitivity. The excitation wavelength (λex) was set at 275 nm, while the emission wavelengths (λem) were 354 nm and 445 nm. UV absorption spectra were recorded using the Shimadzu UV-1800 double-beam UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Kyoto, Japan). High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) was performed using a JEOL JEM-2100 microscope at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Surface charge analysis of WB-n-CQDs was carried out using a particle sizing analyzer (Model 770 AccuSizer, Santa Barbara, California, USA). Surface functional groups were identified using a Bruker TENSOR 37 FT-IR spectrometer (Bruker Optik GmbH, Ettlingen, Germany). X-ray diffraction (XRD) data were collected using a D2 Phaser XRD diffractometer (Bruker, Germany). Elemental mapping was conducted with a JEOL JSM-IT200 microscope. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using a SPECS Surface Nano Analysis system (Version 4.89.2-r104748, Germany, 2022), equipped with an Al Kα X-ray source operating at 400 W and 15 kV, with a beam diameter of 500 μm. High-energy resolution analysis was conducted with smart background correction and peak fitting using a Lorentzian-Gaussian mixed ratio of 30 %. Elemental analysis was performed using a Rayny EDX-720 instrument (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan) with energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX). Zeta potential analysis of the WB-n-CQDs was conducted with a Malvern Nano ZS (UK).

For the synthesis of WB-n-CQDs, a Tornado domestic microwave oven (900 W, Banha, Egypt), an Elma ultrasonic bath (D-78,224 Singen/HTW), and a refrigerated centrifuge (Sigma 3–16KL, Osterode am Harz, Germany) were used. Precise measurements were made using a digital analytical balance (Radwag AS 220/C/2, Michigan, USA). The pH of the solution was adjusted using a Jenway 3510 standard digital pH meter (Cole-Parmer, Illinois, USA) with a double-junction glass electrode calibrated with standard buffers.

WB used in this study was obtained from the local vegetable market in Tanta, Egypt. The samples were rinsed with distilled water, air-dried. The precursor was then heated at 200 °C for 90 min. The WB-n-CQDs were synthesized using a microwave-assisted method. Initially, 1 g of heated WB powder, 1 g of bismuth oxide, and 1 g of folic acid were suspended in 10 mL of distilled water and mixed thoroughly for 2 min till having a homogenous suspension. The mixture was heated in a domestic microwave at 900 W for 30 s. The resulting solution was centrifuged at 7000 rpm for 15 min, followed by a second centrifugation under the same conditions to remove any remaining unfiltered particles Fig. 1 . The solution was then filtered using a 0.45 μm syringe filter. The final solution was collected in a clean, dry Falcon tube and stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for future use. For preparation of the working stock solution, 5 mL of the WB-n-CQDs solution was transferred to a 50 mL volumetric flask and diluted with distilled water to reach the required volume.

The quantum yield (QY) of WB-n-CQDs was calculated using a single-point approach, applying the equation:
QYCQD = QYST X (FCQD/FST)X(AST/ACQD)X(ηCQD/ηST)2

Here, F denotes the integrated fluorescence intensity, A represents the absorbance, and η is the refractive index of the solvent. Quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 (Φst = 0.54) served as the standard fluorescent reference material. The concentration was adjusted to maintain the absorbance of the standard below 0.05.

To prepare the calibration standards, varying volumes (50–500 μL) of an OSC stock solution (10 μg/mL) were transferred into 5 mL volumetric flasks along with 150 μL of WB-n-CQDs working stock solution and 200 μL of AgNO3 solution (30 μg/mL). The final volume was adjusted with distilled water to achieve OSC concentrations ranging from 0.1 to 1 μg/mL. The solutions were thoroughly mixed, and fluorescence measurements were conducted. The fluorescence intensities (F) were recorded at 354 nm and 445 nm upon excitation at 275 nm. Notably, AgNO3 alone exhibited no significant effect on the fluorescence emission at 354 nm but caused quenching at 445 nm. Upon the addition of OSC to the mixture, both emission peaks were affected—the 354 nm emission was quenched, whereas the 445 nm emission, previously quenched by AgNO₃, was restored. A calibration curve was constructed by plotting the fluorescence intensity ratio (F354 nm/F445 nm) against the corresponding OSC concentrations, where F354 nm and F445 nm represent the fluorescence intensities at 354 nm and 445 nm, respectively.

For the assay of the dosage form (1600 mg OSC/tablet), ten tablets were weighed, ground, and homogenized. An average weight equivalent to 0.1 tablet was transferred into a 100 mL volumetric flask, followed by the addition of drops of nitric acid and then 40 mL of 0.1 M HCl. The mixture was sonicated for 30 min with intermittent shaking every 5 min until complete dissolution. After cooling to room temperature, the volume was brought up to 100 mL with 0.1 M HCl, yielding a stock solution of 1.6 mg/mL. A secondary diluted stock solution (16 μg/mL) was prepared by diluting the primary solution with distilled water. Aliquots of this working stock sample solution were then transferred into 5 mL volumetric flasks to prepare samples for analysis.

This study demonstrates an innovative, biocompatible, and straightforward microwave-assisted method for synthesizing WB-n-CQDs, offering an efficient and environmentally friendly alternative to conventional techniques. The experimental variables considered to affect the preparation process were the weights of WB (g), the time of microwave irradiation (s), the time of WB grinding (min), the duration of heating by oven (min) and doping effect. These variables were carefully investigated to establish the best conditions for the fabrication purpose.

Various precursor weights (0.1–1.25 g) were tested using a microwave at 900 W for 30 s, with 1.00 g identified as the optimal weight (Fig. 2
A). The microwave irradiation time was also optimized, ranging from 10 to 60 s, with 30 s proving to be ideal (Fig. 2 B). The maximum excitation and emission wavelengths were recorded at 275 nm and 354 nm, respectively. To enhance selectivity, the effect of heating the precursor was investigated by subjecting WB to 200 °C for 0–90 min. The results indicated a gradual shift in the maximum excitation wavelength (275–338 nm) and emission wavelength (354–445 nm), with pronounced changes after 90 min of heating (Fig. 2 C-F). Grinding the precursor for different durations (0–8 min) was also studied, but no significant impact on the emission intensity was observed. Although heating the precursor introduced dual excitation and emission peaks, the intensity of these peaks was lower than that of WB-n-CQDs prepared without heating. To improve emission characteristics, doping was employed. Folic acid was used as a dopant, significantly enhancing the intensity of the second emission peak at 445 nm (Fig. 3 ) after excitation at 275 nm. Additionally, bismuth oxide was introduced as a second dopant to boost the intensity of the first emission peak at 354 nm (Fig. 3) at the same excitation wavelength. The optimal preparation method involved WB heated at 200 °C for 90 min, combined with folic acid and bismuth oxide as dopants, using a microwave-assisted technique. This approach resulted in WB-n-CQDs exhibiting dual emission peaks at 354 nm and 445 nm. Additionally, the prepared WB-n-CQDs exhibit excellent optical features, characterized by blue fluorescence and remarkable stability.

The synthesized WB-n-CQDs, when combined with silver nitrate (Ag-WB-n-CQDs), serve as a reliable nano-probe for the determination of OSC in both pure and pharmaceutical tablet forms with high quantum yield of 0.21. This study not only introduces an innovative approach for OSC sensing in pharmaceutical samples but also offers a sustainable solution for utilizing biomass waste. Multiple preparation cycles demonstrated consistent fluorescence intensity, confirming the reproducibility of the method.

The fluorescence spectrum of the synthesized WB-n-CQDs was recorded to characterize their optical properties. The WB-n-CQDs exhibit an absorption peak at 275 nm, attributed to π-π* transitions, with maximum emission observed at an excitation wavelength of 354 nm. The HR-TEM image in (Fig. 4
A) reveals that the WB-n-CQDs are homogeneously distributed, monodispersed, and nearly spherical, with an average diameter of 3–5 nm (Figure S1). The monodispersity is attributed to negative surface charges.

Zeta potential analysis determined a value of −36.9 mV, indicating the presence of negatively charged surface functional groups such as carboxyl (-COOH), hydroxyl (-OH), and cyano (-CN) groups. Elemental composition and surface morphology were further analyzed through EDX elemental mapping (Fig. 5
A-I), confirming the presence of carbon (C), oxygen (O), phosphorus (P), sulfur (S), bismuth (Bi), sodium (Na), and potassium (K). The absence of impurities in the EDX spectrum indicates the purity of the synthesis process, while elemental mapping (Fig. 5 B-H) demonstrates uniform distribution of these elements.

FT-IR analysis (Fig. 4 B) was conducted to identify functional groups on the WB-n-CQDs. The spectrum exhibited characteristic bands at 3400 cm−1 for -OH stretching, 1640 cm−1 for -C = C stretching, 2090 cm−1 for metal-carbonyl stretching, and 2300 cm−1 for -SH stretching, confirming the presence of these functional groups on the WB-n-CQDs' surfaces [21].

The XRD spectrum of WB-n-CQDs, shown in (Fig. 4 C), provides insights into their structural features and crystallinity. The WB-n-CQDs typically exhibit either a graphitic crystalline structure (sp2) or an amorphous structure (mixed sp2/sp3) due to the presence of sp2 carbon in their core. The crystallinity of the WB-n-CQDs depends on the proportion of graphitic carbon domains relative to amorphous regions. In (Fig. 4 C), the XRD spectrum displays a broad peak around the 2θ range of 30° This broad peak indicates that the WB-n-CQDs have low crystallinity, which is characteristic of these materials due to the incorporation of oxygen-containing functional groups during synthesis. Additionally, the peak broadening suggests that the graphitic domains are small, consistent with the nanoscale size of the WB-n-CQDs [22].

Additionally, XPS provides valuable information about the surface functional groups of WB-n-CQDs. In Fig. 6
A, the XPS survey spectrum displays distinct peaks at binding energies of 284.7 eV and 531.7 eV, corresponding to C1s and O1s, respectively. A high-resolution scan of the C1s region, shown in Fig. 6 B, highlights carbons in various chemical environments. Specifically, peaks appear at 285 eV for CO bonds, 284.8 eV for CC or C = C bonds, 283 eV for sp2 carbon, and 284 eV for metal carbides. Similarly, Fig. 6 C focuses on the O1s core-level peaks, revealing signals at 530 eV, 531.5 eV, and 533 eV, which correspond to metal oxides, metal carbonates, and organic CO bonds, respectively.

Finally, in the N1s region, shown in Fig. 6 D, the spectrum indicates nitrogen in different chemical environments. Peaks at approximately 400 eV and 397 eV represent CNH2 and metal nitrides, respectively. This analysis confirms the diverse functional groups present on the surface of the WB-n-CQDs [23].

The UV spectrum of WB-n-CQDs, AgNO3, and Ag-WB-n-CQDs (Figure S2A), show that the UV absorbance of WB-n-CQDs at 275 nm remained unchanged upon adding AgNO3. This suggests that fluorescence quenching occurs, either through static or dynamic mechanisms, likely due to spectral overlap effects. To explore this further, the effect of temperature was examined, revealing dynamic quenching at the emission wavelength of 445 nm after excitation at 275 nm. The Stern-Volmer plot [24] (Figure S2B) displayed good linearity at varying AgNO3 concentrations at two other temperatures, 30 °C and 40 °C. It was observed that increasing the temperature reduced the Stern-Volmer quenching constant (Ksv), which can be attributed to the dissociation of the formed complex. The Stern-Volmer equation used is as follows:
F
0
F
= 1 +
K
s v
[ Q ]
Where F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensities of WB-n-CQDs in the absence and presence of AgNO3 at 445 nm, respectively, Ksv is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, and [Q] is the concentration of AgNO3.

In order to evaluate the effectiveness of the fabricated WB-n-CQDs fluorescent probes for quantifying OSC, it is crucial to optimize two key parameters: pH and the volume of WB-n-CQDs. This optimization process involves systematically analyzing each parameter independently while maintaining the other constant. The fluorescence intensity ratio F354 nm/F445 nm was utilized as a standard to refine the experimental parameters. The initial study employed a solution containing 0.2 μg/mL of OSC, 150 μL of the working stock solution of WB-n-CQDs, and 0.2 μg/mL of AgNO3, with distilled water used as the solvent.

The stability of the WB-n-CQDs fluorescent probe is essential for ensuring the method's robustness. To assess the stability of WB-n-CQDs and identify the optimal pH value for maximum fluorescence emission, their fluorescence performance was investigated at various pH conditions. The study focused on a pH range from 2.0 to 6.0, utilizing hydrochloric acid to decrease pH and sodium hydroxide to elevate it. The results, illustrated in Fig. 7 , demonstrate a significant reduction in the fluorescence intensity at pH levels below 3 for both 354 and 445 nm emission wavelengths. Fig. 7 also illustrates an increase in emission intensity at 354 and 445 nm emission wavelengths as the pH rises, with fluorescence remaining relatively stable after reaching a pH of 5.5. These results indicate that Ag-WB-n-CQDs exhibit instability in highly acidic conditions. The lower fluorescence intensity is likely due to the protonation of hydroxyl groups on the surface of WB-n-CQDs, resulting in hydrogen bond formation and aggregation. Consequently, pH 5.5 was selected for WB-n-CQDs superior fluorescence intensity and stability.

The study focused on examining how varying volumes of working stock solution of WB-n-CQDs, specifically between 50 and 150 μL, affected the fluorescence intensity at 354 and 445 nm. The results showed that the highest fluorescence intensity for both wavelengths was achieved at a volume of 150 μL of WB-n-CQDs. Increasing the volume beyond 150 μL resulted in fluorescence intensity exceeding the instrument's maximum detection limit. Consequently, an aliquot of 150 μL WB-n-CQDs was determined to be optimal.
The proposed method was validated according to the ICH guidelines [25], assessing key factors including linearity, range, and quantification limits, accuracy, precision, and robustness.

The fluorescence intensity ratio (F354 nm/F445 nm) was measured using varying OSC within the concentration range of 0.1 – 1 µg/mL at emission wavelengths of 354 and 445 nm to construct the calibration curve. The emission spectrum of Ag-WB-n-CQDs was also recorded under optimal conditions. Fig. 8 demonstrates the restoration of the fluorescence quenching effect of AgNo3 at 445 nm and the quenching of the emission at 354 nm by varying concentrations of OSC. The fluorescence intensities exhibited in a ratiometric analysis as the concentration of OSC increased within the examined range and a linear regression equation was statistically derived to determine the OSC concentration.

The method demonstrated a linear response within the concentration range of 0.1–1.0 µg/mL, with a slope of −9.15 ± 0.35 and an intercept of 10.66 ± 0.22. These values indicate a strong linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio (F354 nm/F445 nm) of and the concentration of OSC. The high coefficient of determination (r² = 0.992) further supports the accuracy and consistency of the linear regression of the method used in this analysis. Sensitivity was evaluated by determining the limits of quantification (LOQ), calculated as 0.1 µg/mL. These findings highlight the method's capability to reliably quantify OSC at low concentrations, ensuring its suitability for precise analytical applications

The accuracy of an analytical method indicates the closeness of the measured results to the true value. To assess accuracy, four concentrations of oyster shell calcium within the linear range were analyzed in triplicate, and the accuracy was determined by calculating the percentage recovery. As presented in Table 1 , the recovery percentages for OSC using the proposed method were within the acceptable range of 98–102 %. Precision was evaluated by measuring both intraday and interday variations. Intraday precision involved triplicate analyses of four OSC concentrations within the linear range on the same day, while interday precision was assessed using the same procedure over three consecutive days. The results, summarized in Table 1, reveal that the %RSD values were below 2 %, confirming the method's high precision.

The robustness of the method was assessed by introducing slight, intentional variations to the parameters and observing their impact on the fluorescence response. The tested parameters included pH (adjusted to 5.5 ± 0.2), incubation time (altered in 1-minute increments), emission wavelength (maintained at 354/445 ± 2.0 nm), and excitation wavelength (set to 275 ± 2.0 nm). The results, as summarized in Table 2 , demonstrated that these minor modifications did not influence the method's performance, confirming its robustness.

The study examined a range of commonly used additives and excipients in pharmaceutical formulations, including acacia gum, potato starch, maize starch, wheat starch, sucrose, fructose, maltose, and lactose, to assess their potential influence on the fluorescence properties of Ag-WB-n-CQDs. The findings revealed that these excipients had an insignificant impact on the fluorescence intensity of WB-n-CQDs. Additionally, Vitamin D3 (VitD3), frequently incorporated as a secondary active component alongside OSC, was evaluated and found to have no effect on WB-n-CQDs, further confirming its selectivity (Figure S3). Furthermore, the impact of several metal ions, including AgNO₃, MnSO₄, MgCl₂, AlCl₃, NiCl₂, SrCl₂, ZnCl₂, BaCl₂, CaCl₂, CdCl₂, CrCl₃, Hg₂Cl₂, Pb(C₂H₃O₂)₂, CuCl₂, FeCl₃, and Bi₅O(OH)₉(NO₃)₄, was investigated to evaluate their potential influence on the sensitivity of WB-n-CQDs. The results demonstrated that while most metal ions exhibited no significant effect, Hg₂Cl₂, Pb(C₂H₃O₂)₂, CuCl₂, FeCl₃, and Bi₅O(OH)₉(NO₃)₄ induced notable changes in the fluorescence emission of WB-n-CQDs (Figure S4). However, these salts are not expected to affect the results in real applications, as they are not co-formulated or co-administered with oyster shell calcium. This was further confirmed by the high accuracy and precision of the proposed method in both the validation study and the analysis of real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the n-CQDs method for dosage form analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The analyte concentration in the dosage form was determined by preparing a stock solution of OSC tablets at a concentration of 1.6 mg/mL. This stock solution was further diluted to produce a working solution of 16 μg/mL. A final OSC concentration of 0.2 μg/mL was obtained by diluting the working solution and adding WB-n-CQDs and AgNO3. The method's applicability was validated through the standard addition approach, yielding an average recovery rate of 99.59 % with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 0.05 %, as detailed in Table S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Greenness and practicality assessment of the proposed method
ce:section.ce:para: The environmental sustainability of the ratiometric method was systematically evaluated using established tools designed for green chemistry assessment, including ComplexMoGAPI [26,27] and AGREEprep [28]. Additionally, the practicality of the method was analyzed using the BAGI [29] and the CACI index [30], which assessed its applicability and efficiency. The evaluation results, depicted in (Fig. 9 ), underscore the method’s overall greenness. The ComplexMoGAPI analysis indicated a predominantly green profile, except for parameter 15 (waste treatment), as the entire prepared sample is classified as waste following Spectrofluorometric analysis. The BAGI pictogram further corroborates the method’s practicality. However, the method has some limitations, such as providing single-component analysis only, and requiring multiple manual preparation steps before analysis, as highlighted by AGREEprep. Despite these minor concerns, the overall assessment demonstrates that the ratiometric method is environmentally friendly, practical, and reliable for its intended applications.

Recognizing the efficacy of the spectrofluorometer as an affordable analytical instrument, we aimed to develop a straightforward, precise, and rapid fluorometric approach utilizing WB-n-CQDs as natural fluorescent probes to quantify OSC in (1600 mg OSC/tablet). The application of WB-n-CQDs offers an innovative, environmentally friendly method for measurement. From a financial standpoint, these probes are synthesized manually in our laboratory through a straightforward microwave-assisted process, which greatly reduces both the cost and time required for the entire measuring procedure. Moreover, the sensitivity of the WB-n-CQDs method is high, featuring a LOQ of 0.1 µg/mL, which exceeds that of atomic absorption spectrophotometry (0.05 mM = 5.55 µg/mL) [9], capillary electrophoresis (0.01 mM = 1.11 µg/mL) [10], and ion-selective electrodes (1.48 mM = 164.28 µg/mL) [11]. Consequently, the Ag-WB-n-CQDs technique is beneficial for OSC measurement in tablets because of its enhanced sensitivity and ease of use.

This study introduces an innovative, environmentally friendly microwave-assisted synthesis method for fabricating n-CQDs from WB, a biomass waste material. The prepared WB-n-CQDs, doped with folic acid and bismuth oxide, exhibit excellent optical properties, including dual-emission peaks at 354 nm and 445 nm, high fluorescence stability, and remarkable repeatability. The XRD analysis reveals that the WB-n-CQDs exhibit an amorphous characteristic, which aligns well with the results from the TEM analysis. The existence of functional groups on the surface of the WB-n-CQDs was validated through FTIR spectra and surface analysis using XPS spectroscopy. The results demonstrated excellent optical properties of the synthesized WB-n-CQDs, which have shown emission-dependent fluorescence behavior and remarkable photostability, making them effective as a fluorescent probe. The developed WB-n-CQDs-based fluorescent probe, in combination with AgNO3, demonstrates high sensitivity and selectivity for OSC measurement in pharmaceutical formulations. The Ag-WB-n-CQDs were applied to OSC analysis in the ranges of 0.1 to 1.0 μg/mL under optimum conditions, where the integrated intensity of fabricated probe fluorescence (F354/F445) versus concentration levels demonstrated an acceptable linear correlation, with a good recovery percentage ranging from 98 % to 102 % and the corresponding relative standard deviations lied between 0.86 and 1.64. The results of the present research not only emphasized the excellent capabilities of WB-n-CQDs but also revealed the potential of biomass waste as a crucial resource for advanced n-CQDs applications, thus advocating for sustainable and environmentally conscious analytical strategies.

Mahmoud Hamed: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Noha Ibrahim: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Conceptualization. Alaa Bedair: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Conceptualization. Fotouh R. Mansour: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Conceptualization. Samy Emara: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Walaa Zarad: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.talo.2025.100492.
